summary:
The Armenian Genocide was the systematic mass killing and deportation of Armenians living in the Ottoman Empire, carried out by the Ottoman government led by the Committee of Union and Progress (Young Turks) beginning in 1915 and continuing through the early 1920s. The killings occurred in the context of World War I, with Ottoman authorities using wartime conditions and security concerns as justification for the forcible removal and destruction of the Armenian population. Estimates of those killed range from 600,000 to 1.5 million people, with the higher estimates more widely accepted by genocide scholars.

The violence took several forms: direct massacres by military and paramilitary units; forced death marches across the Syrian desert with deliberate denial of food and water; drowning operations in the Black Sea and Euphrates River; and mass burnings of entire village populations. Armenian men of military age were typically killed first; women, children, and the elderly were subjected to the death marches to the Syrian desert. The killings were accompanied by the destruction of Armenian cultural and religious institutions and the confiscation of Armenian property.

The genocide is widely recognized by historians, genocide scholars, and more than thirty national governments, including the United States (which formally recognized it in 2021) and France. The Turkish government has historically rejected the characterization of the events as genocide, acknowledging that large numbers of Armenians died but attributing the deaths to wartime conditions, disease, and intercommunal violence rather than deliberate state policy. This denial has been a persistent source of diplomatic tension between Turkey and Armenia, and between Turkey and countries that have formally recognized the genocide.

The Armenian Genocide was one of the first genocides of the twentieth century and has significantly influenced the development of international law on genocide and crimes against humanity. It provided a historical reference point for the drafting of the United Nations Genocide Convention of 1948.
